tate of Califorllia 
Fair Political Practices Commissio1l. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Roger M. Settlemire 
Attorney at Law 
Post Office Box 675 
Chester, California 96020 
Dear Mr. Settlemire: 
July 19, 1976 
1 
This is in response to your letter of July 6 concerning the 
possibility of the existence of a conflict of interest of one of 
the members of the Board of Directors of the Feather River Hospi-
tal District. 
As we discussed on the telephone, the Political Reform Act of 
1974's conflict of interest provisions, Government Code §87l00, et 
seq., are not to be construed as placing any kind of limitation on 
either the ability of any individual to hold public office, or on 
the ability of any political subdivision to contract with private 
entities. 
Rather, the provisions of the Act are aimed at the res-
ponsibility of an individual public official to disqualify himself 
from the decision making process under certain defined circumstances. 
Government Code §87l00 provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local government 
shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to 
use his official position to influence a governmental deci-
sion in which he knows or has reason to know he has a finan-
cial interest. 
("Financial interest" is further defined in Government Code §87l03 
and by regulations adopted by this Commission, 2 Cal.Adm. Code 
§§18702, 18703 and 18704.) 
Thus, it is clear that abstention of the Director, as you refer 
to in your letter, coupled with that Director's not "participating 
in" or "attempting to use his official position to influence" the 
cited decisions of the District suffices to meet that Director's 
obligations under the Political Reform Act.ll 
I would further call

ROGER M. SETTLEMIRE 
State of California 
ATTORNEY AT L.AW 
P. O. BOX 675 
CHESTER. CALIFORNIA 96020 
(916) 256-3131 
July 6, 1976 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Attention: 
Mr. Kenneth Goshorn 
Re: 
Feather River Hospital District 
Request for Opinion on possible 
conflict of interest of Bank 
Manager as member of Board of 
Directors 
Dear Mr. Goshorn: 
As counsel for the Feather River Hospital District, 
I have been directed to request an opinion concerning the 
possibility of a conflict of interest of one of the members 
of the Board of Directors, which Director is also the manager 
of the local branch of Lloyds Bank California. 
As you probably 
know, Lloyds Bank was formerly known as First western Bank 
prior to its acquisition by Lloyds of London. 
Lloyds Bank 
California has its main office in San Francisco and has numerous 
branches allover the state of California. 
By way of background information, the Hospital 
District is in a sparsely populated area of about 2,500 residents, 
and the only bank located within the District is the branch of 
Lloyds Bank of which the Director is the manager. 
Under such 
circumstances, the Board of Directors of the District has been 
inclined in the past to do all banking with the local branch of 
Lloyds Bank as a convenience and in order to "keep the business 
in the area". 
The branch has about a dozen employees and banking 
with the branch is considered helpful to the local economy. 
The possibilities of conflict of interest concern the 
following situations: 
(1) 
In the first situation, the District has 
been doing all of its routine banking, such 
as maintaining its checking and savings 
accounts and obtaining certificates of deposit, 
etc., with the local branch of Lloyds Bank~ 
that the District has done so for many years 
and prior to the time that the Director was 
cted to the Board; that the Director has 
tained on any vote in which banking with the 
local branch is involved; 


state of California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Attention: 
Mr. Kenneth Goshorn 
Re: 
Feather River Hospital District 
Request for Opinion on possible 
conflict of interest of Bank 
Manager as member of Board of 
Directors 
July 6, 1976 
Page 2 
(2) 
In the second situation, the District has 
borrowed various sums of money over the years 
from the local branch; that the District now 
finds itself in a position where a substantial 
sum of money (about $25,000.00) must be borrowed 
on "tax anticipation notes" on a short term 
basis (15 months or less) 
st tax revenues 
and other income for the current year; that the 
District invites bids from all other banks in 
plumas County; that in all matters involving 
such borrowing, the Director has abstained from 
voting; 
(3) 
In the third situation, the local branch 
financed the purchase of a mobile doctor's 
office for a doctor in private practice within 
the District; that the doctor later left the 
area and is reported to have taken bankruptcy 
and the local branch repossessed the mobile unit; 
that the financial stability of the Hospital 
District depends to a large extent on having 
enough doctors practicing in the District to 
place enough patients in the Hospital to bring 
in sufficient income to maintain the Hospital 
(as property taxes would comprise only a small 
percentage of the necessary income); 
that after 
months of being unsuccessful in recruiting a 
doctor, the District decided to set up a medical 
clinic for outpatients as a Hospital District 
function, to enter into a contract with a doctor 
to practice in the medical clinic, to guarantee 
the doctor a minimum annual salary of $35,000, 
and to provide all services for him; that in the 
small community of Greenville, the only suitable 
and logical location for the doctor's office 
appeared to be the mobile unit repossessed by 
the local branch; the Hospital District has been 
renting the mobile unit from the local branch on 


state of California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Attention: 
Mr. Kenneth Goshorn 
Re: 
Feather River Hospital District 
Request for Opinion on possible 
conflict of interest of Bank 
Manager as member of Board of 
Directors 
July 6, 1976 
Page 3 
a month-to-month basis, but the District 
will soon have to enter into a more permanent 
arrangement for the medical clinic office, 
either with the local branch on a lease-purchase 
or obtain some other facility; that the Hospital 
District has encouraged the local branch for 
many months to keep the mobile unit in the area 
for a new doctor, either for private doctor's 
office or the medical clinic operated by the 
Hospital District; that the District would not 
be able to obtain suitable facilities for 
doctor's offices at a comparable expense if the 
Bank re-sold the unit to someone else and it 
had to be moved to another community out of 
the area; that the Director has abstained from 
voting in all matters pertaining to the mobile 
unit and plans to continue to do so. 
Would you please render an opinion as to whether 
there is, or would be, a conflict of interest in the three 
situations set forth above, and, if so, whether such conflict 
of interest would disqualify the Director on such matters or 
disqualify him from serving as a Director. 
Your' consideration of these matters would be sincerely 
appreciated. 
Sincerely, 
RMS:llt

sections.question: Would there be a conflict of interest in the three situations set forth above, and, if so, whether such conflict of interest would disqualify the Director on such matters or disqualify him from serving as a Director. The three situations involve: (1) routine banking services at Lloyds Bank where the Director is manager and has abstained from voting; (2) borrowing funds via tax anticipation notes where the Director has abstained from voting; and (3) renting a mobile medical clinic office unit from Lloyds Bank where the Director has abstained and plans to continue abstaining from voting.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions are aimed at the responsibility of an individual public official to disqualify himself from the decision making process under certain defined circumstances. Government Code §87100 provides that no public official shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Thus, abstention of the Director, coupled with that Director's not 'participating in' or 'attempting to use his official position to influence' the cited decisions of the District suffices to meet that Director's obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a bank manager serving on a hospital district board has a conflict of interest in three scenarios involving banking services, borrowing, and rental of a mobile clinic unit from that bank, and whether abstention from voting is sufficient to comply with conflict of interest provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that abstention from voting and non-participation in decision-making on matters where the Director has a financial interest is sufficient to comply with the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions. The Act does not limit individuals from holding public office or subdivisions from contracting with private entities; it requires only that officials disqualify themselves when they have a financial interest in the decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (Settlemire letter of July 6) and FPPC response (July 19 letter). Extraction focuses on FPPC response as primary document. Question and conclusion sections identified from the incoming request letter and response letter respectively. Document quality is fair with some OCR artifacts but core content is legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would there be a conflict of interest in the three situations set forth above, and, if so, whether such conflict of interest would disqualify the Director on such matters or disqualify him from serving as a Director. The three situations involve: (1) routine banking services at Lloyds Bank where the Director is manager and has abstained from voting; (2) borrowing funds via tax anticipation notes where the Director has abstained from voting; and (3) renting a mobile medical clinic office unit from Lloyds Bank where the Director has abstained and plans to continue abstaining from voting.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions are aimed at the responsibility of an individual public official to disqualify himself from the decision making process under certain defined circumstances. Government Code §87100 provides that no public official shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Thus, abstention of the Director, coupled with that Director's not 'participating in' or 'attempting to use his official position to influence' the cited decisions of the District suffices to meet that Director's obligations under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request regarding potential conflicts of interest for a bank manager serving on a hospital district board who participates in banking, borrowing, and rental decisions. The Commission advises that abstention from voting and non-participation in decision-making regarding matters where the Director has a financial interest satisfies the requirements of the Political Reform Act.
